items.correct: The sudden release of built-up stress along faults at plate boundaries as rocks slip past each other, generating seismic waves.
items.misconception: Earthquakes are caused by magma rising up to the surface.
items.random_wrong: Earthquakes are caused by the Earth's core overheating.
items.why: Students conflate volcanic and seismic processes. Most earthquakes are tectonic (stress release at faults), not volcanic — magma movement is a separate process and most quakes occur at non-volcanic boundaries.

items.q: What is the difference between the focus and epicentre of an earthquake?
items.correct: The focus (hypocentre) is the underground point where the earthquake originates; the epicentre is the point on the surface directly above the focus.
items.misconception: The focus and epicentre are the same point.
items.random_wrong: The focus is at the centre of the Earth and the epicentre is on the surface.
items.why: Students treat the two terms as synonymous or place the focus deep at the Earth's core. The focus is just below the epicentre, typically only a few to tens of kilometres deep.

items.q: Why do earthquakes of similar magnitude cause different amounts of damage?
items.correct: Damage depends on depth, distance from population, building quality, ground type (liquefaction risk), time of day, and emergency preparedness — not just magnitude.
items.misconception: Bigger magnitude always means more deaths.
items.random_wrong: All earthquakes of magnitude 7 cause the same damage everywhere.
items.why: Magnitude measures energy; impact depends on context. A magnitude 7 in a poor, dense city kills thousands; the same offshore or in a wealthy area may kill no one — a key development comparison.

items.q: At which boundary do plates pull APART?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Constructive. “Destructive” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is the point on the SURFACE directly above an earthquake called?
items.why: It's Epicentre. “Focus” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Each whole number step on the Richter scale represents how many times more energy released?
items.why: The real figure is 32. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
